title: Editorial: Redefining ‘the Artist’ through New Narratives
content:
Dr Anna-Maria von Bonsdorff, Director, Ateneum Art Museum / Finnish National Gallery

25 March 2026

Finnish art is now more visible and present internationally than ever. Museums, such as New York’s Metropolitan Museum of Art, are acquiring Finnish art and Finnish art is present in many exhibitions in major museums around Europe and the US. This interest is growing. However, it also means that, as museum professionals and curators at the Finnish National Gallery, we are facing the fact that it is even more important that knowledge of Finnish history is expertly disseminated to our counterparts abroad, in addition to explaining the context the artists were and are working in. It also means a shift away from the emphasis on the solitary genius artist.

In this issue of FNG Research, there is a significant focus on ‘the artist’ and in several articles we highlight the many well-known names in Finnish art history, as well as new research and fresh approaches to these artists’ narratives. On the one hand we need to emphasise the context for our partners abroad and on the o

title: Conservation of Four Sketches, by Akseli Gallen-Kallela, for the Frescoes at the Finnish Pavilion at the 1900 Paris World’s Fair
content:
Ilona Osara BA, Paper Conservator, Finnish National Gallery

—

In 1898, three Finnish architects, Eliel Saarinen, Armas Lindgren and Herman Gesellius won the competition to design Finland’s pavilion at the 1900 Paris World’s Fair. Since 1896, Akseli Gallen-Kallela (1865–1931) had been creating paintings inspired by the Kalevala, the Finnish national epic, each work contributing to the emerging Finnish national identity. He was commissioned to paint murals for the Finnish Pavilion’s Iris Room and to decorate the arches of the pavilion, giving him an opportunity to present his art to an international audience.[1]

The Finnish Pavilion at the 1900 Paris World’s Fair became a landmark project that showcased Finland as a modern and distinct nation. Featuring the finest Finnish art, design, and industry, it earned widespread acclaim.[2] Gallen-Kallela himself was awarded a gold medal by the World’s Fair for his contributions.[3]

title: The Collection Exhibition as Fantasy
content:
Satu Oksanen MA, Curator, Museum of Contemporary Art Kiasma / Finnish National Gallery

Translation: Liisa Muinonen-Martin

—

This essay is published in Saara Hacklin, Saara Karhunen, Satu Oksanen (eds.), A Dream in Four Colours. Kiasma’s Collection Exhibition. A Museum of Contemporary Art Publication 182/2026. Helsinki: Museum of Contemporary Art Kiasma, 2026.

This article grew out of the notes I made while curating ‘A Dream in Four Colours’, a collection exhibition at the Museum of Contemporary Art Kiasma in 2026, and draws on my reflections from all the time I have spent with the museum’s art collection over the years I have worked in Kiasma. It is an exercise in thinking within the museum institution and in asking questions about how art is exhibited and viewed, and it explores the collection and the collection exhibition, as well as the gallery spaces in which it is displayed.

‘A Dream in Four Colours’ represents one possible perspective among many on the Finnish National Gallery’s contemporary art collection,[1] which is itself a con

title: The Renaissance of Fanny Churberg’s Art and Artistry
content:
Dr Hanne Selkokari, Curator, Finnish National Gallery / Ateneum Art Museum

—

Today, a memorial exhibition dedicated to Fanny Churberg will open to invited guests at the Stenman Konstsalong [Art Salon]. The exhibition comprises 74 selected works by this rarely seen female artist.[1]

The new era or renaissance of the art of Fanny Churberg (1845–1892), as Riitta Konttinen has called it, began with the ’Fanny Churberg’ exhibition, organised by Stenman’s Konstsalong [Art Salon], in 1919.[2] The exhibition aroused the interest of Finnish art critics, art dealers and art collectors alike. Organising exhibitions of individual artists who had been ‘forgotten’ or were ‘unknown’ was part of the commercial strategy of the art dealer Gösta Stenman (1888–1947). The art historian Camilla Hjelm, who has studied Stenman, has noted that with Stenman a new power emerged on the Finnish art scene of the early 20th century, as he succeeded in re-evaluating prevailing views and created his own stable of artists.

title: Eero Nelimarkka – The City-dweller from Southern Ostrobothnia
content:
Dr Riitta Ojanperä, Director of Collections Management, Finnish National Gallery

Translation: Maija Timonen

This article first appeared in the book Eero Nelimarkka, edited by Marja Lahelma, published by the Finnish National Gallery / Ateneum Art Museum, 2026

—

The Professor, painter Eero Nelimarkka is one of the best-known artists in the history of independent Finland. Above all, he is the patriarchal depicter of the landscape, people and habitats of the plains. […] Working alternately in Helsinki and Ostrobothnia, Eero Nelimarkka’s work is also known beyond art circles.

So begins the Finnish Broadcasting Company’s television documentary about the life and work of the artist Eero Nelimarkka (1891–1977), completed in the summer of 1974.[1] The documentary, Eero Nelimarkka – Kasvo [Eero Nelimarkka – Face], follows the 82-year-old painter in Alajärvi in Ostrobothnia, at the Nelimarkka Museum, which he founded. From the 1930s onwards, his renown grew and, after the war years, so did the number of people buying his work. Farmhouse in

title: Longing for Originality and the Fear of Primitiveness: People, Nature and Racialisation in Finnish Modern Art, 1915–1930
content:
Dr Timo Huusko, Chief Curator, Ateneum Art Museum / Finnish National Gallery           

—

The questions in my doctoral thesis partly stem from my years studying at the University of Turku. I am referring to Tyko Sallinen’s (1879–1954) artworks and the wonder they evoked in me at the time. I mainly pondered why Sallinen was regarded as such an important artist. My interest was reignited in 2014 when I visited the art exhibition ‘1914’, held at the Latvian National Gallery, in Riga.

The exhibition displayed works created in the so-called peripheral states or border countries during the collapse of the German, Russian, and Austro-Hungarian empires. One of these peripheral states was Finland, and among others the exhibition featured paintings by Tyko Sallinen. I noticed that many works from different countries in the exhibition shared a certain pathos in the way the artists depicted the essence of their own people. I deliberately say ‘their own people’ here because the artists’ obvious aim was to present the distinctive characteristics of their people and the deep r

title: Going Global – Pekka Halonen and Helene Schjerfbeck Step into the Limelight
content:
Gill Crabbe, FNG Research

Much loved in the Nordic region, two of Finland’s great artists, Helene Schjerfbeck and Pekka Halonen, have received a boost to their international status with exhibitions in two major museums. Ateneum Art Museum Director Anna-Maria von Bonsdorff discusses the strategies behind the scenes with Gill Crabbe

—

With monographic exhibitions of two of Finland’s most important artists from the long 19th-century taking place in major museums on the world stage, the Ateneum Art Museum is having a peak moment. As the museum’s Director Anna-Maria von Bonsdorff points out, the exhibitions of Albert Edelfelt in Paris and Akseli Gallen-Kallela previously in Vienna are followed by Pekka Halonen at the Petit Palais in Paris and Helene Schjerfbeck at the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York, marking a unique moment in the history of Finnish art. The two new shows can be seen as a high point of the Ateneum’s Classics for the World project, an initiative that began in 2022 with the Edelfelt project and has evolved as a highly effective strategy for showcasing the best of Finnish ar

title: Art History as Thought and Conviction: The Living Legacy of Salme Sarajas-Korte
content:
Dr Marja Lahelma, Chief Curator, Finnish National Gallery / Ateneum Art Museum

—

The year 2025 marked the centenary of the eminent Finnish art historian, Professor Salme Sarajas‑Korte (1925–2017). To honour this anniversary, a jubilee seminar was held at the Ateneum Art Museum on 9 December 2025. The event highlighted Sarajas‑Korte’s significant and multifaceted contributions to art-historical research, the museum field, and contemporary art discourse. Central to her work was an interest in the literary and philosophical dimensions of art, as well as in phenomena that, particularly in Finland, had remained in the shadow of nationally oriented and modernism‑focused art-historical writing. At the same time, she played an active role in shaping institutions of contemporary art and exhibition practices.

The jubilee seminar was organised in collaboration with the Ateneum Art Museum and the research project ‘Cultural Amnesia and the “Golden Age” of Finnish Art’ (University of Helsinki, funded by the Research Council of Finland). My warmest thanks go to all the organising partners who made this remarkable day possible. Fo

title: Research Interns Selected by the Finnish National Gallery for 2026
content:
Two research interns have been selected for the FNG Research internship programme for 2026. The decisions were made based on the applications and the following points were underlined:

  • Priority was given to students whose applications were based on a concrete and defined part of the FNG collections and especially to previously unstudied and/or topical materials.
  • The preparation of the working plan and the research questions related to the chosen collections material or other topical questions linked with the museum’s collections.

The FNG Research internship programme has two aims. The Finnish National Gallery wishes to enhance the study of its collections, including artworks, archives, and objects. At the same time it wishes to support students who choose to study subjects in a museum context, based on physical collections and objects, archive material and data.

The research interns at the Finnish National Gallery for 2026 are:

Ano Kurki, University of Helsinki
Materials and collections in the Finnish National Gallery from the 1960s related to the esoteric

title: Editorial: The Allure of the Nordic
content:
Dr Riitta Ojanperä, Director of Collections Management, Finnish National Gallery

November 26, 2025

This autumn has seen some important landmarks in the Finnish National Gallery’s international activities aiming at making our rich collections increasingly well known internationally. Two monographic exhibitions of foremost artists in Finnish art history are taking place – Pekka Halonen (1865–1933), at the Petit Palais in Paris and Helene Schjerfbeck (1862–1946), at the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York. Both are organised in collaboration with the Ateneum Art Museum / Finnish National Gallery and both are supported by significant loans from public and private collections. Bridging and contextualising local art histories and art-historical questions in general has led the Finnish National Gallery to expand its intellectual networks and its professional impact in many ways.

The lives and works of the two Finnish artists open several perspectives concerning issues that are often considered to be typically Nordic. Among those is the northern natural environment and Nordic artists’ supposedly specific relationship with nature,
